title: AI-Driven Vulnerability Prioritization in Practice

modules.title: Why CVSS Scores Alone Are Failing Security Teams
modules.sub: Exposing the blind spots of static severity scoring systems
modules.description: Learners examine real-world breach cases where high-CVSS vulnerabilities went unpatched while lower-scored flaws were actively exploited. They analyze the structural limitations of CVSS — including its lack of threat context and environmental nuance — and articulate why a score-only approach creates dangerous prioritization gaps. By the end, learners can make a concrete case for why supplemental intelligence is necessary.

modules.title: How AI Models Estimate Exploitation Probability from Real-World Data
modules.sub: Understanding the signals AI uses to predict attacker behavior
modules.description: Learners explore how AI prioritization models ingest inputs such as exploit code availability, dark web chatter, patch lag trends, and asset exposure to estimate the likelihood a vulnerability will be weaponized. They trace the logic from raw signal to risk score, evaluating which model features carry the most predictive weight. This builds the conceptual foundation needed to critically assess any AI prioritization tool's outputs.

modules.title: Feeding AI Prioritization Tools: Data Quality, Bias, and Coverage
modules.sub: What goes in determines what risks get seen or missed
modules.description: Learners audit the data sources that feed an AI prioritization pipeline — threat intelligence feeds, asset inventories, and patch telemetry — and identify where gaps or biases can skew results. They work through scenarios in which poor data coverage causes high-risk vulnerabilities to be systematically underscored. The module equips learners to assess data readiness before deploying or trusting an AI prioritization system.

modules.title: Hands-On: Comparing AI-Assisted vs. Manual Triage Workflows
modules.sub: Testing AI recommendations against analyst judgment in practice
modules.description: Learners work through a simulated vulnerability backlog, triaging it first manually and then using an AI-assisted tool, and compare the outcomes across speed, accuracy, and missed critical findings. They document where the AI surface prioritization differed from their own judgment and investigate the reasons behind each divergence. This direct comparison builds calibrated trust — and healthy skepticism — toward AI-generated recommendations.

modules.title: Integrating AI Prioritization Into Existing Patch Management Pipelines
modules.sub: Connecting AI outputs to tickets, SLAs, and remediation teams
modules.description: Learners map how AI prioritization scores can be operationalized within existing tools such as vulnerability scanners, ITSM platforms, and SOAR workflows, configuring handoff points and escalation thresholds. They design an integration plan that preserves human review checkpoints while reducing time-to-remediation for high-risk findings. The result is a practical workflow blueprint they can adapt to their own organizational environment.

modules.title: Measuring and Validating Prioritization Accuracy Over Time
modules.sub: Building feedback loops that keep AI recommendations trustworthy
modules.description: Learners define metrics for evaluating whether the AI's top-priority vulnerabilities were genuinely the highest-risk ones, using retrospective data on actual exploits and incident records. They design a lightweight validation cadence — including false-positive and false-negative tracking — that surfaces model drift before it erodes operational trust. This module instills the discipline of treating AI prioritization as a hypothesis to be tested, not a verdict to be accepted.

modules.title: Governance and Accountability When AI Drives Remediation Decisions
modules.sub: Maintaining human ownership in an AI-assisted security process
modules.description: Learners examine the accountability questions that arise when an AI recommendation influences which vulnerabilities get patched — and which do not — including audit trail requirements and explainability obligations. They draft a governance policy that assigns clear human ownership at each decision point and specifies how AI outputs must be documented for compliance and incident review purposes. The module closes by stress-testing that policy against a simulated audit and a post-incident review scenario.

synopsis: Security teams drown in vulnerability alerts, and traditional severity scoring leaves the most exploitable flaws buried under noise — a gap that attackers have learned to exploit. This course shows how AI-powered prioritization models use threat intelligence, exploit availability, and environmental context to surface the vulnerabilities that actually get weaponized, and how to integrate those signals into real workflows.
rationale: AI-assisted vulnerability prioritization is rapidly becoming a foundational security operations capability, yet practitioners lack structured guidance on how these models work, where they fail, and how to operationalize them responsibly.
learning_outcome: Learners can evaluate, configure, and critically interrogate an AI-assisted vulnerability prioritization tool and integrate its outputs into a defensible, auditable patch management workflow.
source_signals: Vulnerability Exploitation Probability Metric Proposed by NIST, CISA Researchers
Small models also found the vulnerabilities that Mythos found
nearest_existing: Every generation of programmers has faced a tool that rewrites what review even means
